message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 20
Recent changes: Added optional third parameter {isFollowUp} and 15% follow-up discount

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

content.text: I'll start the Refactor Phase by reading the necessary files.

content.content:
1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted inline `{ type: string }` to exported `type Item` for function signature clarity
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `items: Item[]` reads better than `items: { type: string }[]`
6	- Naming established: `Item` (type alias for item objects), `calculateBasePremium` (function)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced if/else conditional with `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table (`Record<string, number>`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- declarative map makes type-to-premium mapping explicit and treats all item types symmetrically
11	- Naming established: `BASE_PREMIUM` (module-level constant mapping item type to base premium value)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: added `Readonly` wrapper to `BASE_PREMIUM` type annotation
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- communicates immutability of configuration constant and enables compile-time mutation prevention
16	- Naming established: --
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): attempted destructuring `items[0]` into named binding but reverted -- mass increased from 8 to 15 with only marginal clarity gain; code already at local optimum after 3 prior refinement cycles
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- Change: replaced `Readonly<Record<string, number>>` with `as const` assertion and derived `ItemType = keyof typeof BASE_PREMIUM` union type
23	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- `Item.type` is now a precise union instead of `string`, enabling compile-time validation of item types
24	- Naming established: `ItemType` (union type: `"sword" | "amulet" | "staff" | "potion" | "rune"`)
25	
26	## After cycle 6
27	- Change: replaced imperative `for` loop + mutable `sum` with `items.reduce(...)` single expression
28	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- reduce expresses "fold to sum" directly and eliminates 2 assignments (mass 28 -> 18)
29	- Naming established: --
30	
31	## After cycle 7
32	- Change: extracted `isComponentBlock(items)` helper, introduced `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM`, and `COMPONENT_TYPES` named constants
33	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- inline condition `items.length === 3 && items.every(...) && BASE_PREMIUM[...] === 25` was opaque; extracted function makes block-detection logic self-documenting; mass +5 justified by clarity gain
34	- Naming established: `isComponentBlock` (predicate), `BLOCK_SIZE` (3), `BLOCK_PREMIUM` (60), `COMPONENT_TYPES` (ReadonlySet of component item types eligible for block discount)
35	
36	## After cycle 8
37	- Change: restructured `isComponentBlock` with early-return guard clause and extracted `blockType` named binding from repeated `items[0].type`
38	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- guard clause separates precondition from core logic; `blockType` names the concept more clearly than indexed access; mass +1 justified by clarity
39	- Naming established: `blockType` (local binding in `isComponentBlock` for the type that defines the block)
40	
41	## After cycle 9
42	- Change: removed dead `isComponentBlock` function (no longer called after green phase replaced it with inline group-by-type block detection in `calculateBasePremium`)
43	- Rationale: Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- dead code adds cognitive load and 11 mass units for zero value
44	- Naming established: --
45	
46	## After cycle 10
47	- Change: merged cursed surcharge loop into item iteration loop; introduced `cursedSurcharge` named accumulator separate from `total`
48	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named separation of base premiums (`total`) vs item-specific modifiers (`cursedSurcharge`) clarifies composition; reduced loops from 3 to 2
49	- Naming established: `cursedSurcharge` (accumulator for item-level cursed modifier, kept separate from base premium total)
50	
51	## After cycle 11
52	- Change: extracted `itemSurcharge(item: Item): number` helper; collapsed `cursedSurcharge`/`enchantmentSurcharge` into single `totalSurcharge` accumulator in main function
53	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- separates per-item surcharge logic from grouping/block-discount logic; names the concept; simplifies main loop body from 3 accumulators + 2 conditionals to 1 accumulator + 1 call
54	- Naming established: `itemSurcharge` (function computing all item-level surcharges for one item), `totalSurcharge` (accumulator in main function)
55	
56	## After cycle 12
57	- Change: renamed `calculateBasePremium` to `calculatePremium`; extracted `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` as named constants
58	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- function now computes full premium with policy-wide modifiers, not just base; magic numbers replaced with self-documenting constants
59	- Naming established: `calculatePremium` (renamed from `calculateBasePremium`), `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (2), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (0.2)
60	
61	## After cycle 13
62	- Change: extracted inline magic numbers in `itemSurcharge` to named constants `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
63	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- consistent with existing pattern of named rate/threshold constants; mass +24 justified by clarity
64	- Naming established: `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)
65

content.text:
All 20 tests passing. Let me now proceed with the Refactor Phase systematically.

## Step 0: Architecture Memory Loaded

I've read `architecture-notes.md` -- 13 prior cycles of refinements documented.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

**Naming Evaluation**:
- Current name: `calculatePremium`
- Function purpose: Computes the insurance premium for a list of items, applying item-level surcharges, block discounts, loyalty discounts, first-insurance surcharge, and follow-up discounts.
- Question: Does "calculatePremium" clearly reveal this intent?
- Assessment: Yes -- the function computes a premium, and "calculatePremium" is a clear, domain-appropriate name.
- Current helper name: `itemSurcharge`
- Helper purpose: Computes per-item surcharges (cursed + high-enchantment) based on item base premium.
- Assessment: Yes -- "itemSurcharge" clearly describes what the function returns.

Decision: Keep current names -- both `calculatePremium` and `itemSurcharge` accurately describe their roles.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Let me calculate the current code mass for the implementation.

**Current Code Mass** for `calculatePremium`:
```
function calculatePremium(items: Item[], customer?: { yearsWithMHPCO: number }, options?: { isFollowUp?: boolean }): number {
  const groups = new Map<ItemType, number>();       // invocation: 2 (Map constructor), binding: 1 (groups)
  let totalSurcharge = 0;                           // assignment: 6, constant: 1 (0)
  for (const item of items) {                       // loop: 5, binding: 1 (item)
    groups.set(item.type, (groups.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1);  // invocations: 4 (set, get, ??, +), constant: 2 (0, 1)
    totalSurcharge += itemSurcharge(item);           // assignment: 6, invocation: 2 (itemSurcharge)
  }

  let total = 0;                                    // assignment: 6, constant: 1 (0)
  for (const [type, count] of groups) {             // loop: 5, binding: 2 (type, count)
    if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === BLOCK_SIZE) {  // conditional: 4, invocations: 4 (has, &&, ===, BLOCK_SIZE ref)
      total += BLOCK_PREMIUM;                       // assignment: 6
    } else {                                        // (covered by conditional)
      total += count * BASE_PREMIUM[type];          // assignment: 6, invocations: 2 (*, [])
    }
  }
  let policyPremium = total + totalSurcharge;       // assignment: 6, invocation: 2 (+)
  if (customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) {  // conditional: 4, invocations: 4 (&&, >=, .yearsWithMHPCO, threshold)
    policyPremium *= 1 - LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE;     // assignment: 6, invocations: 2 (-, *), constant: 1 (1)
  }
  policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE;  // assignment: 6, invocations: 2 (*, +)
  if (options?.isFollowUp) {                        // conditional: 4, invocation: 2 (?.)
    policyPremium *= 1 - FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE;   // assignment: 6, invocations: 2 (-, *), constant: 1 (1)
  }
  return policyPremium;                             // binding: 1
}
```

Let me be more precise with a careful count:

Constants: 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1 = 6 constants x 1 = 6
Bindings: groups, totalSurcharge, item, total, type, count, policyPremium = 7 bindings x 1 = 7
Invocations: new Map, set, get, ??(coalescing), +(add 1), itemSurcharge, +(surcharge), has, &&(block), ===(block), *(count*base), [](index), +(total+surcharge), &&(customer), >=(years), *=(loyalty), -(1-rate), *=(firstIns), +(firstIns), ?.(options), *=(followUp), -(1-rate) = 22 invocations x 2 = 44
Conditionals: if(COMPONENT_TYPES...), if(customer...), if(options...) = 3 conditionals x 4 = 12
Loops: for(items), for(groups) = 2 loops x 5 = 10
Assignments: totalSurcharge=0, totalSurcharge+=, total=0, total+=(block), total+=(normal), policyPremium=, policyPremium*=(loyalty), policyPremium+=(firstIns), policyPremium*=(followUp) = 9 assignments x 6 = 54

Total Mass for `calculatePremium`: 6 + 7 + 44 + 12 + 10 + 54 = **133**

**Current Code Mass** for `itemSurcharge`:
```
function itemSurcharge(item: Item): number {
  const base = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];             // binding: 1, invocation: 2 ([])
  let surcharge = 0;                                // assignment: 6, constant: 1
  if (item.cursed) {                                // conditional: 4
    surcharge += base * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE;      // assignment: 6, invocation: 2 (*)
  }
  if (item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD) {  // conditional: 4, invocations: 4 (!==, &&, >=)
    surcharge += base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE;  // assignment: 6, invocation: 2 (*)
  }
  return surcharge;
}
```

Constants: 0, undefined = 2 constants x 1 = 2
Bindings: item, base, surcharge = 3 bindings x 1 = 3
Invocations: [](BASE_PREMIUM), *(cursed), !==, &&, >=, *(enchantment) = 6 invocations x 2 = 12
Conditionals: if(cursed), if(enchantment) = 2 conditionals x 4 = 8
Loops: 0
Assignments: surcharge=0, surcharge+=(cursed), surcharge+=(enchantment) = 3 assignments x 6 = 18

Total Mass for `itemSurcharge`: 2 + 3 + 12 + 8 + 0 + 18 = **43**

**Combined Total Mass: 176**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass -- All 20 passing.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent

Looking at the `calculatePremium` function, I notice the body is growing complex with multiple sequential concerns:
1. Group items and compute surcharges (loop 1)
2. Compute block/base premiums (loop 2)
3. Apply policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first-insurance, follow-up)

The function body mixes three concerns. However, extracting helpers at this stage might be premature -- the function is still readable linearly.

One thing I notice: the policy-wide modifier application section has a specific pattern that could be clarified. The line `policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` is semantically equivalent to `policyPremium *= 1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, which would be consistent with how the loyalty and follow-up discounts are expressed (as multiplications). Let me check if this improves clarity.

Currently:
- Loyalty: `policyPremium *= 1 - LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` -- multiplicative form
- First insurance: `policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` -- additive form
- Follow-up: `policyPremium *= 1 - FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE` -- multiplicative form

The inconsistency hurts readability. Converting the first insurance surcharge to the same multiplicative pattern (`policyPremium *= 1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`) would make all three modifiers consistent. This improves Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) by showing that all policy modifiers are percentage-based multipliers.

### Rule 3: No Duplication -- No obvious duplication.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements -- The additive form `policyPremium += policyPremium * RATE` uses 2 invocations and 1 assignment, while `policyPremium *= 1 + RATE` uses 2 invocations, 1 constant, and 1 assignment. Mass difference is negligible (+1 for the constant 1, same otherwise). But consistency is a clarity gain.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I will convert the first-insurance surcharge from `policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` to `policyPremium *= 1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` to match the pattern used by the other two policy modifiers.

content.text: The change introduced floating-point precision issues. The original `policyPremium += policyPremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` and the new `policyPremium *= 1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` are mathematically equivalent but produce slightly different floating-point results due to IEEE 754. I need to revert this change since it breaks tests.

content.text:
Good, reverted and all 20 tests passing again. The consistency refactoring was a good idea but breaks due to floating-point precision differences. Let me try a different refactoring approach.

Let me look at the function more carefully for another improvement opportunity.

Looking more carefully at the code, I see another opportunity. The `customer` parameter check on line 54 uses `customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= ...` which can be simplified to use optional chaining: `customer?.yearsWithMHPCO !== undefined && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= ...`. Actually, the simplest and most consistent approach -- given that `options?.isFollowUp` already uses optional chaining on line 58 -- would be to use optional chaining for the customer check too.

Wait, the current check `customer && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` already works correctly because if `customer` is undefined, the `&&` short-circuits. But it could be written more concisely as:

```typescript
if (customer !== undefined && customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD) {
```

Actually, the existing form is already idiomatic. Let me look for a different improvement.

I notice that `itemSurcharge` uses a mutable accumulator pattern (`let surcharge = 0; ... surcharge += ...; return surcharge;`) for only two possible addends. This could be simplified by computing the result directly without mutation. Let me try replacing the mutable accumulator with a direct expression.

content.text:
All 20 tests passing after the refactoring. Let me now calculate the new mass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored `itemSurcharge` Code Mass**:
```typescript
function itemSurcharge(item: Item): number {
  const base = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];                     // binding: 1, invocation: 2 ([])
  const cursed = item.cursed ? base * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0;  // binding: 1, conditional: 4, invocation: 2 (*), constant: 1 (0)
  const enchantment =                                       // binding: 1
    item.enchantment !== undefined && item.enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
      ? base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE              // conditional: 4, invocations: 4 (!==, &&, >=, *), constant: 1 (undefined)
      : 0;                                                  // constant: 1 (0)
  return cursed + enchantment;                              // invocation: 2 (+)
}
```

Constants: 0, undefined, 0 = 3 constants x 1 = 3
Bindings: item, base, cursed, enchantment = 4 bindings x 1 = 4
Invocations: [](BASE_PREMIUM), *(cursed rate), !==, &&, >=, *(enchantment rate), +(return) = 7 invocations x 2 = 14
Conditionals: ?:(cursed), ?:(enchantment) = 2 conditionals x 4 = 8
Loops: 0
Assignments: 0 (all const, no mutations)

New Total Mass for `itemSurcharge`: 3 + 4 + 14 + 8 + 0 + 0 = **29**

Previous mass for `itemSurcharge`: **43**
Mass Change: 43 -> 29 (delta -14)

The key improvement: eliminated all 3 assignments (saving 18 mass), added 1 more constant and 1 more binding and 1 more invocation (adding 4 mass). Net savings of 14 mass units.

Combined Total Mass: 133 + 29 = **162** (was 176, delta -14)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Keep name: `itemSurcharge` -- accurately describes the function's purpose
- Keep name: `calculatePremium` -- accurately describes the main function's purpose
- Replaced mutable accumulator pattern (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...; return surcharge`) with immutable named bindings (`const cursed = ...; const enchantment = ...; return cursed + enchantment`)
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The named bindings `cursed` and `enchantment` make explicit which surcharge component contributes what -- the reader can see the composition directly in `return cursed + enchantment`
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Eliminated 3 assignments (mass cost 18), replacing them with cheaper constants and bindings
- Mass reduced from 43 to 29 for `itemSurcharge` (delta -14)
- Note: Attempted consistency refactoring of first-insurance surcharge line to match multiplicative pattern of other modifiers, but reverted due to floating-point precision differences causing test failures

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: Replaced mutable accumulator in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`cursed`, `enchantment`), improving both clarity and mass
**Mass Change**: 176 -> 162 (delta -14), specifically `itemSurcharge` went from 43 -> 29
**Tests**: All 20 passing

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

- Naming established: `CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (0.3)

## After cycle 14
- Change: replaced mutable accumulator (`let surcharge = 0; surcharge += ...`) in `itemSurcharge` with immutable named bindings (`const cursed`, `const enchantment`) and direct return
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) -- named bindings make surcharge composition explicit; eliminating 3 assignments reduced mass from 43 to 29
- Naming established: `cursed` (local const in `itemSurcharge` for curse surcharge amount), `enchantment` (local const for high-enchantment surcharge amount)
